content.text: A data steward roles and accountability model for agentic AI defines the specific human responsibilities for ensuring that data used by AI agents is classified correctly, handled according to policy, governed throughout its lifecycle, and accountable to a named individual when issues arise. It extends traditional data stewardship—which focuses on data quality, cataloguing, and access control—to cover the additional accountability requirements introduced by AI agents: who is responsible for what the agent retrieves, how it processes data, and what outputs it produces.

The model assigns three types of roles at multiple levels of the organisation: strategic roles that set policy and accept ultimate accountability (CDO, DPO), domain roles that apply policy to specific data sets or AI products (Domain Steward, AI Product Steward, Platform Steward), and operational roles that implement controls on a day-to-day basis (Data Owner, Agent Product Owner, Platform Engineer). Each role has defined responsibilities, decision rights, and escalation paths.

AI agents blur the traditional accountability boundaries of data governance. In a conventional workflow, a human employee is accountable for every decision that touches data. In an agentic workflow, the agent makes data access and processing decisions autonomously. When a data governance failure occurs—a wrong classification, an unauthorised access, a residency violation—there must be a human accountable for the failure and empowered to remediate it. Without a defined accountability model, failures are either attributed to 'the AI' (which cannot be held accountable) or result in organisational disputes about which team is responsible.

Regulators and auditors increasingly ask not just 'what controls exist' but 'who is accountable for this control'. The GDPR concept of accountability (Article 5(2)) requires that the controller not only comply but also demonstrate compliance—which requires named individuals with documented responsibilities. The EU AI Act requires that high-risk AI systems have designated points of contact. A defined stewardship model produces this documentation naturally.
When a data governance incident occurs, the accountability model immediately identifies the responsible person without requiring an organisational discussion.
Named stewards with documented responsibilities satisfy GDPR's accountability principle and the EU AI Act's designated-contact requirements.
Mapping responsibilities explicitly reveals gaps where no one owns a critical control, enabling remediation before an incident occurs.
The model defines the escalation path for every decision type, preventing governance decisions from stalling because no one knows who can resolve them.
Auditors asking 'who is responsible for X' can be answered immediately from the accountability model rather than through ad-hoc investigation.

In small organisations, one person may hold multiple steward roles, creating conflicts of interest. Minimum-viable stewardship models may combine roles while maintaining separation of accountability for critical decisions.

Requiring data steward sign-off for every agent deployment or tool addition slows delivery. Tiered approval thresholds—where low-risk actions proceed without steward review—balance governance with speed.
Agentic AI frequently spans multiple data domains in a single workflow. Identifying which Domain Steward is accountable for a cross-domain agent action requires a clear jurisdictional model.
Named accountability depends on individuals staying in their roles. A steward who leaves without a documented handover creates accountability gaps.
The accountability model comprises six roles organised in three layers. Each role definition includes scope, key responsibilities, and decision rights.

The Chief Data Officer and Data Protection Officer share ultimate accountability for the organisation's data governance framework. The CDO owns the data strategy, classification taxonomy, and stewardship model. The DPO owns regulatory compliance, data protection impact assessments (DPIAs), and is the regulator's point of contact.
Sets the data classification taxonomy and tier definitions.
Approves the lineage metadata standard and stewardship policy.
Accepts accountability for the overall data governance framework.
Signs DPIAs for high-risk AI deployments.

The Domain Steward is accountable for all data within a specific data domain (e.g., Customer Data, Financial Data, HR Data). In the context of agentic AI, the Domain Steward approves which AI agents may access their domain's data and at which classification tier.
Maintains the classification record for their domain's data assets.
Approves or denies AI agent access to domain data.
Reviews and approves any change to the data tier of a domain asset.
Is the primary contact for data quality issues in their domain.
The AI Product Steward is accountable for the data governance aspects of a specific AI product or agent family. This is an AI-specific role that did not exist in traditional data stewardship.
Owns the system prompt policy and version history for their AI product.
Approves model selections for use in the product.
Reviews high-tier output samples for policy compliance.
Is the primary contact for tool governance decisions affecting the product.
The Platform Steward is accountable for the infrastructure and tooling that supports agentic AI data governance across the organisation.
Owns the regional deployment control architecture.
Maintains the lineage metadata standard and lineage pipeline.
Data Owner and Agent Product Owner (Operational Layer)

Data Owners are business-side individuals who own specific data assets within a domain. Agent Product Owners are the individuals responsible for a specific deployed agent's day-to-day operation. Both roles implement the policies set by the steward layer and are the first line of incident response.

Platform Engineers implement the technical controls that enforce governance policies: lineage tracking, classification enforcement, network policies, and residency controls. They are on-call for platform-level incidents.

attrs.alt: Data stewardship accountability model for agentic AI diagram

content.text: Establish the accountability model before the first production deployment, not after the first incident.
Data protection authorities expect to find named individuals with documented responsibilities; the accountability model is the primary artifact.
As the number of agents grows, the accountability model prevents responsibility gaps from opening up between applications.
When teams reorganise, the accountability model identifies which role assignments must be updated before the change takes effect.

Start by mapping existing data governance roles to the new model. Many organisations already have Domain Stewards in some form for traditional data assets. The new AI-specific roles (AI Product Steward, Platform Steward) need to be created or assigned to appropriate individuals.
Inventory existing data governance roles and identify where they map to the model's six role types.
Identify gaps: typically the AI Product Steward and Platform Steward roles are new and require new assignments or formal expansion of existing roles.
Document the RACI matrix for the governance activities relevant to the organisation's agentic AI deployment.
Publish role definitions, assignments, and the RACI matrix in the internal governance documentation portal.
Build the steward assignments into relevant approval workflows: tool approval tickets should route to the named Platform Steward; data access approvals should route to the named Domain Steward.
Establish a role-handover procedure: any steward leaving their role must document the handover to their successor before departure.
Review role assignments quarterly to ensure they remain current and that all roles are covered.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class GovernanceActivity(str, Enum):
    CLASSIFY_DATA = "classify_data"
    APPROVE_AGENT_ACCESS = "approve_agent_access"
    APPROVE_MODEL = "approve_model"
    CONDUCT_DPIA = "conduct_dpia"
    APPROVE_TOOL = "approve_tool"
    PRODUCE_ATTESTATION = "produce_attestation"
    INCIDENT_RESPONSE = "incident_response"
    DATA_SUBJECT_REQUEST = "data_subject_request"

class StewardRole(str, Enum):
    CDO_DPO = "cdo_dpo"
    DOMAIN_STEWARD = "domain_steward"
    AI_PRODUCT_STEWARD = "ai_product_steward"
    PLATFORM_STEWARD = "platform_steward"
    AGENT_PRODUCT_OWNER = "agent_product_owner"

@dataclass
class StewardAssignment:
    role: StewardRole
    name: str
    email: str
    domain: Optional[str] = None  # None for org-wide roles
    effective_date: str = ""
    backup_email: str = ""  # Mandatory backup contact

@dataclass
class AccountabilityModel:
    organisation: str
    stewards: list[StewardAssignment] = field(default_factory=list)

    # RACI: R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted, I=Informed
    _raci: dict[GovernanceActivity, dict[StewardRole, str]] = field(
        default_factory=lambda: {
            GovernanceActivity.CLASSIFY_DATA: {
                StewardRole.CDO_DPO: "A", StewardRole.DOMAIN_STEWARD: "R",
                StewardRole.AI_PRODUCT_STEWARD: "C", StewardRole.PLATFORM_STEWARD: "C",
            },
            GovernanceActivity.APPROVE_AGENT_ACCESS: {
                StewardRole.CDO_DPO: "A", StewardRole.DOMAIN_STEWARD: "R",
                StewardRole.AI_PRODUCT_STEWARD: "C", StewardRole.AGENT_PRODUCT_OWNER: "C",
            },
            GovernanceActivity.APPROVE_MODEL: {
                StewardRole.CDO_DPO: "A", StewardRole.AI_PRODUCT_STEWARD: "R",
                StewardRole.PLATFORM_STEWARD: "C",
            },
            GovernanceActivity.INCIDENT_RESPONSE: {
                StewardRole.CDO_DPO: "A", StewardRole.DOMAIN_STEWARD: "R",
                StewardRole.PLATFORM_STEWARD: "R", StewardRole.AGENT_PRODUCT_OWNER: "R",
            },
        }
    )

    def get_accountable(self, activity: GovernanceActivity) -> list[StewardAssignment]:
        """Return the accountable steward(s) for a governance activity."""
        raci = self._raci.get(activity, {})
        accountable_roles = [role for role, code in raci.items() if "A" in code]
        return [s for s in self.stewards if s.role in accountable_roles]

    def get_responsible(self, activity: GovernanceActivity) -> list[StewardAssignment]:
        """Return the responsible steward(s) for a governance activity."""
        raci = self._raci.get(activity, {})
        responsible_roles = [role for role, code in raci.items() if code == "R"]
        return [s for s in self.stewards if s.role in responsible_roles]

    def escalation_path(self, activity: GovernanceActivity) -> str:
        responsible = self.get_responsible(activity)
        accountable = self.get_accountable(activity)
        r_names = ", ".join(s.name for s in responsible)
        a_names = ", ".join(s.name for s in accountable)
        return f"Responsible: {r_names} → Accountable: {a_names}"

# Example
model = AccountabilityModel(organisation="Acme Corp")
model.stewards = [
    StewardAssignment(StewardRole.CDO_DPO, "Jane Smith", "dpo@acme.com",
                      effective_date="2024-01-01", backup_email="cdo@acme.com"),
    StewardAssignment(StewardRole.DOMAIN_STEWARD, "Bob Lee", "bob.lee@acme.com",
                      domain="Customer Data", effective_date="2024-01-01"),
    StewardAssignment(StewardRole.AI_PRODUCT_STEWARD, "Alice Chen", "alice.chen@acme.com",
                      domain="Support Agent", effective_date="2024-03-01"),
    StewardAssignment(StewardRole.PLATFORM_STEWARD, "Sam Patel", "sam.patel@acme.com",
                      effective_date="2024-01-01"),
    StewardAssignment(StewardRole.AGENT_PRODUCT_OWNER, "Raj Kumar", "raj.kumar@acme.com",
                      domain="Support Agent", effective_date="2024-03-01"),
]

activity = GovernanceActivity.APPROVE_AGENT_ACCESS
print(f"Escalation for {activity.value}:")
print(model.escalation_path(activity))

A global pharmaceutical company introduces an agentic AI system for clinical trial data analysis. Before deployment, the governance team maps out steward roles: the CDO owns the data governance framework; a Clinical Data Domain Steward is assigned from the data science team and is responsible for classifying clinical trial datasets and approving agent access; an AI Product Steward from the clinical AI team is assigned to the trial analysis agent family; and the Platform Steward is an existing data platform team lead who adds AI governance responsibilities to their scope. The accountability model is published in the company's internal Confluence with named individuals and their backup contacts. When a regulatory authority questions whether the AI system correctly classified a dataset containing patient identifiers, the Clinical Data Domain Steward is the named responder with documented accountability for that classification decision. The inquiry is resolved in two weeks because the steward has a complete record of the classification decision, its rationale, and the lineage records showing which agent sessions accessed the dataset.
Every steward role must have a backup who is empowered to act when the primary is unavailable. A single named individual with no backup is a single point of failure for governance decisions.
A steward who is named as accountable but lacks the organisational authority to make the decisions they are accountable for cannot fulfill the role.
Naming a Domain Steward accountable for all data in their domain without giving them tools to monitor agent access to that data makes accountability nominal rather than real.
A RACI matrix that is a document but not integrated into approval workflows means the right people are not actually involved in governance decisions.
Treating stewardship as a compliance exercise:
Steward roles that exist only to satisfy regulatory requirements without real authority or engagement produce checkbox compliance, not genuine governance.
Assign steward roles before first deployment:
Governance gaps that exist from day one of a deployment are much harder to close retroactively than if roles are assigned at the start.
Tool approval workflows, classification review processes, and incident routing should use the steward assignment registry to automatically notify the right person.
Publish a calendar reminder for quarterly role reviews. Verify all roles are covered and all assignments are current.
When a steward leaves their role, they must complete a documented handover that includes current decisions in flight, pending approvals, and open incidents.
Accountable stewards need dashboards and alerts for the areas they are accountable for—Domain Stewards need an agent access log; AI Product Stewards need output quality metrics.

As the number of AI agents and data domains grows, the accountability model scales by adding Domain Stewards per domain and AI Product Stewards per agent family, while the CDO/DPO and Platform Steward layers remain constant. A well-designed tiered approval model means most routine governance decisions are handled at the Domain and Product Steward level without escalating to the CDO/DPO, keeping the strategic layer focused on policy and exception decisions.
For large domains, a Lead Domain Steward can delegate specific responsibilities (e.g., access approvals for lower-tier data) to deputy stewards while retaining accountability.
As the agent fleet grows, manual review of all governance signals is infeasible. Stewards need automated dashboards that surface only anomalies and exceptions requiring their attention.
Workflow platforms for routing governance approvals to the named steward for each decision type.
Data catalog platforms with built-in data steward assignment and workflow features.
Governance platform with data owner and steward assignment capabilities integrated with Microsoft 365 and Azure data services.
On-call management platforms for integrating Platform Steward and Platform Engineer on-call rotations with incident routing.

The steward assignment registry—the record of who holds which role—is a governance-critical document. If it is incorrect, governance decisions route to the wrong person and accountability is misattributed. Protect the registry with access controls that prevent accidental or malicious modification, version it so changes are auditable, and require sign-off from the CDO/DPO for any steward assignment change. Treat the registry as a regulated document, not an operational configuration.

Reliability of governance depends on backup coverage. Every steward role must have a named backup who can act within 24 hours if the primary is unavailable. For Tier 4 Restricted data incidents, the on-call rotation must include stewards who have the authority to make containment decisions, not just platform engineers who can implement technical mitigations.

The data stewardship accountability model assigns six named roles across three layers: strategic (CDO/DPO), domain (Domain, AI Product, Platform Steward), and operational (Data Owner, Agent Product Owner, Platform Engineer).
Each role has defined responsibilities, decision rights, and escalation paths, converting governance from an organisational aspiration into an operational reality.
The RACI matrix makes accountability explicit for each governance activity, eliminating ambiguity when incidents occur or regulatory inquiries arrive.
Steward assignments must be integrated into approval workflows, not just documented in a wiki, to be operationally effective.
Quarterly role reviews and documented handover procedures prevent accountability gaps from opening as the organisation evolves.
